content: hello
Hi there
continued from https://categorytheory.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/229136-category-theory/topic/code.20wtfs
which perl talk do you mean?
One sec, let me find it
This one https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=noQcWra6sbU
perl is cheating for wat
i used to know some perl years ago
You'd think so, but then this is beyond reasonable even for perl
hmm, we'll see :eyes:
god it's 30 minutes
is there a fun part i can skip to ;-;
Not really
I mean I guess it depends how much knowledge of the esotheric interactions of perl features you have
maybe ill watch it later then
@Stelios Tsampas that's not at all my complaint, though
my complaint is that the presentation is full of misleading info for people writing code in dynlangs today

im not whining that shes saying php has security features when haskell is more secure, im whining that shes saying php has security features when standard python and ruby libraries have far safer patterns
im also whining that shes spending significant time discussing ruby's taint feature as if it were a major point to consider on ruby when i didnt think it ever got used
like, when i left the talk, i was irritated not as a PL person but as someone who had written web apps in php and python and ruby
(...depending on what counts as "web apps" :slight_smile:)
but anyway, i did think that the part where she actually talked about this new project was kinda interesting and i just wish more of the talk had been about that

sarahzrf said:

im not whining that shes saying php has security features when haskell is more secure, im whining that shes saying php has security features when standard python and ruby libraries have far safer patterns

I don't think she was explicitly comparing php with ruby or advocating for the security of php (which would have been an incredibly, incredibly bizarre thing to do). Shame it left that impression but in any case, I felt there were more serious issues with the presentation.
i mean, she put up php as an example of a language which supposedly has support for sanitization or escaping or something
when in actuality most sql libraries in non-php languages i've ever used do the interpolation for you so that you can't forget to sanitize
so php is actually possibly the worst example you could pick
Can't argue with that! Maybe she didn't know? Not easy to stay up-to-date with every single technology these days...
people have been arguing this point for at least five years, i bet at least ten
I actually like php to code at but it's been historically so, so broken...

sarahzrf said:

people have been arguing this point for at least five years, i bet at least ten

Meaning secure interfaces for SQL databases? Oh yeah, they've been around for ages, few years after SQL injections became widespread. Maybe she wanted a simple example on sanitization? Idk...
and like i said, i dont think anybody uses ruby's taint system, so i dont know why she spent so long talking about it
this is from before i was born https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0248/
the execute function has an optional params argument
(i figured 10 was still a huge underestimate but i didnt wanna go further than i could be confident lol)
10 years sounds about right. The web until around 2008 was comically full of SQL injections. Today's numbers are nothing!
i mean, the tech was clearly around for at very least more than twice as long as that
i wonder how much of the change is people switching from php to this kind of library ;)

It was definitely not widespread, although I can't pinpoint exactly when the first secure db interfaces started popping up. But 2010 is definitely not far off the initial year when things started looking way more secure.

sarahzrf said:

i wonder how much of the change is people switching from php to this kind of library ;)

A lot! In the same spirit, the development of widely-deployable solutions by tech giants like Oracle and Microsoft certainly raised the bar.
i.e. .NET and the Java web stuff, hugely complex beasts that are ultimately more secure and very easy to write code in.
